title: Yet another mysterious permissions problem when running rootless
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
 When running a rocky or alma 8.6 container rootles (on a centos stream 8 host), `yum install openssh` consistently fails
<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

`Error: cpio: chown failed - Inappropriate ioctl for device`

when running yum install openssh in a podman container.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  using podman 4.0.2 on centos stream 8, run` podman run --rm -it rockylinux:8.6 bash`

2. run `yum install openssh`

**Describe the results you received:**

Install will fail with: 

```
wrror: unpacking of archive failed on file /usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign;62c7b29c: cpio: chown failed - Inappropriate ioctl for device
error: openssh-8.0p1-13.el8.x86_64: install failed
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

openssh will install. Error occurs even when openssh is just a dependency (e.g. when installing git). podman therefore about as useful as a chocolate teapot.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.2
API Version:  4.0.2
Go Version:   go1.17.7

Built:      Tue Mar 15 15:15:06 2022
OS/Arch:    linux/amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman-4.0.2-1.module_el8.7.0+1106+45480ee0.x86_6
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

No. Its the version in Centos Stream 8. That's a big deal even if the bug is fixed upstream.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
```
$ ls -ln /usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign
-r-xr-sr-x. 1 0 ssh_keys 330152 Jan 20 23:51 /usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign
```

the file is owned by ID 0:982 (on my system, the GID might be different for you) but you have only 401 IDs available in the user namespace.

One workaround it is to run something like:

```
$ podman run --uidmap 0:0:100 --uidmap 800:100:300 --rm -it rockylinux:8.6 bash
```

a better fix is to allocate more additional IDs to your user (through `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid` then run `podman system migrate`)

Awesome! I completely misunderstood how the id mapping works. Can’t believe how much time I spend on this, really appreciate your help.

Many thanks

Sent from my iPhone

> On Jul 8, 2022, at 4:43 AM, Giuseppe Scrivano ***@***.***> wrote:
> 
> ﻿
> $ ls -ln /usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign
> -r-xr-sr-x. 1 0 ssh_keys 330152 Jan 20 23:51 /usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign
> the file is owned by ID 0:982 (on my system, the GID might be different for you) but you have only 401 IDs available in the user namespace.
> 
> One workaround it is to run something like:
> 
> $ podman run --uidmap 0:0:100 --uidmap 800:100:300 --rm -it rockylinux:8.6 bash
> a better fix is to allocate more additional IDs to your user (through /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid then run podman system migrate)
> 
> —
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub, or unsubscribe.
> You are receiving this because you authored the thread.

@giuseppe I'm having the same issue as OP and am not sure I follow your solution. I likely have some misunderstanding about how podman works but why would the host's `/usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign` be of concern here? I would expect the container to not depend on the host's `ssh-keysign`.

Thanks in advance!

It's not the hosts file but the file that I was attempting to install in the container. The installer - which remember is running inside the podman runtime - attempts to assign a GID outside the range allowed by the ID mapper.

@softwareplumber Thanks, very helpful!

But how do you know what range of mappings to use – or what GID the installer is trying to use? `podman info --debug` reports:
```yaml
  idMappings:
    gidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 10034
      size: 1
    uidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 66563
      size: 1
```
Is it possible to determine a valid `--uidmap` range from this?
you've only one UID and GID available.
